name: Prajnakaragupta
dates_notes: Prajñākaragupta is usually placed in the late eighth to early ninth century by his position after Dharmakīrti and before later Buddhist pramāṇa reception; exact dates are not secure.
core_thesis: Prajñākaragupta is a major commentator in the Dharmakīrti pramāṇa tradition. His *Pramāṇavārttikālaṅkāra* comments on Dharmakīrti's *Pramāṇa-Vārttika* and is especially important for the reception of the *pramāṇa-siddhi* material, where epistemology, Buddhist soteriology, and the status of the Buddha as reliable authority meet. He is also associated with the compact *Sahopalambhaniyama-siddhi*, an argument in the Yogācāra orbit that object and cognition are invariably apprehended together. The roster report marks the witnesses as Sanskrit manuscript/facsimile plus Tibetan rather than clean local etext, so this entry records attested works without claiming on-disk primary text.

engaged_works.title: Pramanavarttika-alankara
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in the later Dharmakīrti commentary period.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely attributed to Prajñākaragupta in the Buddhist pramāṇa commentarial tradition.

ascription_evidence.description: Buddhist pramāṇa tradition transmits the work as Prajñākaragupta's commentary on Dharmakīrti.

engaged_works.summary: A major Sanskrit commentary on Dharmakīrti's *Pramāṇa-Vārttika*. It participates in the technical exposition of inference, perception, apoha, and the *pramāṇa-siddhi* argument for the Buddha's authority. For the timeline it belongs with the Buddhist pramāṇa comparators that later Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, and Vedānta inherit as live opponents or technical sources.

engaged_works.title: Sahopalambhaniyama-siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally associated with Prajñākaragupta; included here because the roster gap report names it with his corpus, but the entry does not rely on a local etext.
engaged_works.summary: A short argument concerning the invariable co-apprehension of cognition and object, used in Yogācāra-oriented debates over whether the object can be established as external to cognition. The work is relevant to the sākāra/nirākāra and Buddhist pramāṇa debates, but this entry keeps the ascription cautious because no clean local witness was verified in this pass.
